shortDescription.text: NoSQL Injection via unsanitized user input in MongoDB $where clause
Insecure Direct Object Reference allowing access to other users' data

message.text: The 'threshold' query parameter is interpolated directly into a MongoDB $where clause without sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript code via the threshold parameter (e.g., `0';while(true){}' ` for DoS, or `1'; return 1 == '1` to bypass filtering). The $where operator evaluates the string as server-side JavaScript, making this a critical NoSQL injection vulnerability. Fix: parse threshold as an integer with parseInt(), validate the range, and use standard MongoDB query operators ($gt) instead of $where.

The userId for allocation lookup is taken from the URL parameter (req.params.userId) instead of the authenticated session. Any logged-in user can access another user's allocation data by changing the userId in the URL path (e.g., GET /allocations/otherUserId). Fix: use req.session.userId instead of req.params.userId to enforce that users can only view their own allocations.

The allocations route is registered as GET /allocations/:userId with only an isLoggedIn middleware check. There is no authorization check to verify the authenticated user matches the requested userId. This enables horizontal privilege escalation where any authenticated user can view any other user's financial allocation data.

The /learn endpoint redirects to a URL taken directly from the query string (req.query.url) without any validation. An attacker can craft a link like /learn?url=https://evil.com to redirect authenticated users to a malicious site for phishing. Fix: validate the redirect URL against an allowlist of trusted domains or restrict to relative paths.
